2:
<list>Environmental impact of electricity from biogas in Italy 
Energy and GHG emission balances were assessed for 3 biogas plants. 
Alternative scenarios: heat valorization, different reference systems for electricity 
Electricity from biogas saves GHG emissions and fossil fuel use. 
Critical factors are: energy crop production, methane losses, feedstock transport.</list>

4: Throughout the years, the attention on the quantification of the en- vironmental impacts derived from agricultural production systems has increased considerably. The agricultural contribution to greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions is undeniable (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate

Change [IPCC], 2007). Agricultural activities are responsible for 405 MtCO2eq per year (10% of the total Europe GHG emissions). Nitrous oxide emissions (from fertilizer application and manure management) represent approximately 210 MtCO2eq, while methane emissions (from enteric fermentation, manure management, and rice cultivation) account for about 195 MtCO2eq (De Cara et al., 2005). Furthermore, considering the European objectives regarding the reduction of fossil fuel consumption and GHG emissions, the production of energy from renewable sources is a priority (European Parliament, 2009, 2010).

Interest in the different agro-energy processes, including biogas pro- duction, is still increasing (González-García et al., 2012). In Europe in 2011, renewable energy sources (RES) produced 676 TWh of electric energy. This figure is equivalent to 20.6% of the gross electricity con- sumption. Among the RES, hydraulic power represents 46.0%, wind power 26.5%, biomass 19.7%, solar power 6.9%, and geothermal power and ocean energies 0.9% (EurObserv'ER, 2012).

In Italy, energy production from RES was already 9% in 2009; in 2010, it was > 10%; and in 2011, it reached 11.6%. In particular, in Italy, 100 TWh per year should be produced by RES to cover the 26% of elec- tric consumption (Ministero Sviluppo Economico, 2012). By the end of 2011, the installed electric power assured the production of 94 TWh per year. In this scenario, the anaerobic digestion (AD) of different feedstock appears to be one of the more promising ways to meet the European objectives (Ministero Sviluppo Economico, 2012).

In Italy, during the past 10 years, agricultural biogas production was considerably increased, and nowadays, more than 520 agricultural bio- gas plants are running mainly in northern regions (Piccinini, 2012). In 2011, biogas produced 3405 GWh of electricity (EurObserv'ER, 2012). Strong public incentives are granted for electricity produced from biogas. From 2013 with the (D.M. 6 July 2012) (Ministero Sviluppo Economico, 2012) the public incentives framework for electricity production from biogas has been updated giving more importance to heat valorization and byproducts utilization. Nevertheless, for the biogas plants put into operation before 31 December 2012 and with electrical power lower than 1 MWe, 280 €/MWhe are granted for the electricity fed into the grid without any consideration regarding heat valorization and byproduct utilization for feeding. Thus, despite the possibility of using biogas for thermal generation or upgrading it to biomethane, due to the incentives, biogas is usually utilized to feed combined heat and power generation plant (CHP) engines and therefore converted into electricity and heat.

Regarding the feeding of digesters, although the AD of animal ma- nure is one of the best techniques for an energetic valorization of these by-products (Angelidaki and Ellegaard, 2003; Edelmann et al., 2005; Maranon et al., 2011; Pantaleo et al., 2013), most biogas plants are fed with energy crops. The cereal silages are the main feedstock for biogas production, both in Italy and in other European countries (Comino et al., 2009; Moriizumi et al., 2012; Patterson et al., 2011; Hartmann, 2006; Dressler et al., 2012). However, biogas plants can use manure in co-digestion with energy crops. The co- digestion of different feedstock results in benefits that come from the AD of manure (energy generation, organic matter stabilization, and reduction of methane emissions during storage) with the incre- ment of the CHP electrical power and the reduction of digester volumes (Bystricky et al., 2010).

Although the AD of agricultural feedstock can be performed with dif- ferent types of biogas plants (Fabbri et al., 2011), the most widespread technology is characterized by mesophilic conditions and single-stage digestion in continuous stirred-tank reactors [CSTR] (Fantozzi and Buratti, 2009a, 2009b).

Biogas production involves important environmental issues, espe- cially global warming, acidification, and eutrophication potentials (Dressler et al., 2012; Meyer-Aurich et al., 2012). Many studies have been conducted concerning the environmental sustainability and performance of biogas production systems (Edelmann et al., 2005; Appels et al., 2011; Tricase and Lombardi, 2012; Pertl et al., 2010; Dressler et al., 2012; Pöschl et al., 2012) as well as biogas conversion into electricity (Hartmann, 2006; Benetto et al., 2010). In particular, special attention has been paid on GHG emissions (Clemens et al., 2006; Rehl and Muller, 2013; Dressler et al., 2012) and energy balance (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007; Benetto et al., 2010; Kimming et al., 2011; Dressler et al., 2012) related to these bioenergy systems. Most of these studies have performed these assessments fol- lowing the life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology, a standardized and holistic method that allows for identifying the environmental
consequences of the life cycle of a product, process, or activity by eval- uating the potential environmental impacts throughout its whole life cycle production chain (ISO 14040, 2006).

The increasing number of biogas plants, especially those larger than 500 kW electrical power, involves a high consumption of energy crops, large transportation distances from both the input side (bio- mass feedstock) and the output side (digestate), and difficulties with thermal energy valorization (Chevalier and Meunier, 2005). The is- sues listed above may play different roles depending on the size of the biogas plant and the type of biomass used (Pöschl et al., 2010, 2012).

This study aims at assessing the sustainability of the electricity pro- duction from biogas considering different types of feedstock and biogas plant sizes. Thus, a full overview of feedstock production as well as of biogas conversion into electricity in Italy must be presented. The sus- tainability has been assessed in terms of the two concerns established by European Union (European parliament and council, 2003): energy demand and GHG emissions throughout their life cycles.

8: Throughout the years, the attention on the quantification of the en- vironmental impacts derived from agricultural production systems has increased considerably. The agricultural contribution to greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions is undeniable (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate

Change [IPCC], 2007). Agricultural activities are responsible for 405 MtCO2eq per year (10% of the total Europe GHG emissions). Nitrous oxide emissions (from fertilizer application and manure management) represent approximately 210 MtCO2eq, while methane emissions (from enteric fermentation, manure management, and rice cultivation) account for about 195 MtCO2eq (De Cara et al., 2005). Furthermore, considering the European objectives regarding the reduction of fossil fuel consumption and GHG emissions, the production of energy from renewable sources is a priority (European Parliament, 2009, 2010).

Interest in the different agro-energy processes, including biogas pro- duction, is still increasing (González-García et al., 2012). In Europe in 2011, renewable energy sources (RES) produced 676 TWh of electric energy. This figure is equivalent to 20.6% of the gross electricity con- sumption. Among the RES, hydraulic power represents 46.0%, wind power 26.5%, biomass 19.7%, solar power 6.9%, and geothermal power and ocean energies 0.9% (EurObserv'ER, 2012).

In Italy, energy production from RES was already 9% in 2009; in 2010, it was > 10%; and in 2011, it reached 11.6%. In particular, in Italy, 100 TWh per year should be produced by RES to cover the 26% of elec- tric consumption (Ministero Sviluppo Economico, 2012). By the end of 2011, the installed electric power assured the production of 94 TWh per year. In this scenario, the anaerobic digestion (AD) of different feedstock appears to be one of the more promising ways to meet the European objectives (Ministero Sviluppo Economico, 2012).

In Italy, during the past 10 years, agricultural biogas production was considerably increased, and nowadays, more than 520 agricultural bio- gas plants are running mainly in northern regions (Piccinini, 2012). In 2011, biogas produced 3405 GWh of electricity (EurObserv'ER, 2012). Strong public incentives are granted for electricity produced from biogas. From 2013 with the (D.M. 6 July 2012) (Ministero Sviluppo Economico, 2012) the public incentives framework for electricity production from biogas has been updated giving more importance to heat valorization and byproducts utilization. Nevertheless, for the biogas plants put into operation before 31 December 2012 and with electrical power lower than 1 MWe, 280 €/MWhe are granted for the electricity fed into the grid without any consideration regarding heat valorization and byproduct utilization for feeding. Thus, despite the possibility of using biogas for thermal generation or upgrading it to biomethane, due to the incentives, biogas is usually utilized to feed combined heat and power generation plant (CHP) engines and therefore converted into electricity and heat.

Regarding the feeding of digesters, although the AD of animal ma- nure is one of the best techniques for an energetic valorization of these by-products (Angelidaki and Ellegaard, 2003; Edelmann et al., 2005; Maranon et al., 2011; Pantaleo et al., 2013), most biogas plants are fed with energy crops. The cereal silages are the main feedstock for biogas production, both in Italy and in other European countries (Comino et al., 2009; Moriizumi et al., 2012; Patterson et al., 2011; Hartmann, 2006; Dressler et al., 2012). However, biogas plants can use manure in co-digestion with energy crops. The co- digestion of different feedstock results in benefits that come from the AD of manure (energy generation, organic matter stabilization, and reduction of methane emissions during storage) with the incre- ment of the CHP electrical power and the reduction of digester volumes (Bystricky et al., 2010).

Although the AD of agricultural feedstock can be performed with dif- ferent types of biogas plants (Fabbri et al., 2011), the most widespread technology is characterized by mesophilic conditions and single-stage digestion in continuous stirred-tank reactors [CSTR] (Fantozzi and Buratti, 2009a, 2009b).

Biogas production involves important environmental issues, espe- cially global warming, acidification, and eutrophication potentials (Dressler et al., 2012; Meyer-Aurich et al., 2012). Many studies have been conducted concerning the environmental sustainability and performance of biogas production systems (Edelmann et al., 2005; Appels et al., 2011; Tricase and Lombardi, 2012; Pertl et al., 2010; Dressler et al., 2012; Pöschl et al., 2012) as well as biogas conversion into electricity (Hartmann, 2006; Benetto et al., 2010). In particular, special attention has been paid on GHG emissions (Clemens et al., 2006; Rehl and Muller, 2013; Dressler et al., 2012) and energy balance (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007; Benetto et al., 2010; Kimming et al., 2011; Dressler et al., 2012) related to these bioenergy systems. Most of these studies have performed these assessments fol- lowing the life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology, a standardized and holistic method that allows for identifying the environmental
consequences of the life cycle of a product, process, or activity by eval- uating the potential environmental impacts throughout its whole life cycle production chain (ISO 14040, 2006).

The increasing number of biogas plants, especially those larger than 500 kW electrical power, involves a high consumption of energy crops, large transportation distances from both the input side (bio- mass feedstock) and the output side (digestate), and difficulties with thermal energy valorization (Chevalier and Meunier, 2005). The is- sues listed above may play different roles depending on the size of the biogas plant and the type of biomass used (Pöschl et al., 2010, 2012).

This study aims at assessing the sustainability of the electricity pro- duction from biogas considering different types of feedstock and biogas plant sizes. Thus, a full overview of feedstock production as well as of biogas conversion into electricity in Italy must be presented. The sus- tainability has been assessed in terms of the two concerns established by European Union (European parliament and council, 2003): energy demand and GHG emissions throughout their life cycles.

10: LCA is a methodological framework for quantifying and assessing the environmental impacts attributable to a product's life-cycle-that is, from raw material acquisition-through the production and use phases to waste management at the end of life, which is known as a "from-cradle-to-grave" analysis (ISO 14040, 2006). LCA is an objective process for evaluating the environmental burdens associated with a product by identifying natural resource consumption and emissions to environmental compartments and for identifying and implementing opportunities to attain environmental improvements. The present study concerns the assessment of electricity production from biogas.
11: As mentioned, the goal of this study is the assessment of the energy and the GHG emission balances of the production of electricity from biogas in northern Italy under different real scenarios based on different feedstocks and biogas plant sizes.

Therefore, following LCA guidelines, three biogas-to-energy pro- cesses have been analyzed and carefully assessed in order to quantify the environmental effects and to identify the environmental key pro- cess (ISO 14040, 2006). Considering that feedstock production or re- covery involves important environmental issues (González-García et al., 2013) particular attention was paid to the agricultural subsystem. The production of an energy crop (maize) in different districts of northern Italy as well as the recovery of pig slurry was evaluated in detail.

The AD of two different feedstocks (pig slurry and maize silage) in three biogas plants located in the Po Valley area and characterized by different amounts of electrical power were assessed in order to iden- tify the system with the highest energetic benefit and environmental friendliness.
12: Biogas production systems have two main functions: i) the digestion of organic material into biogas and ii) the subsequent use as fuel for electricity and/or heat generation. In this study, two different functional units have been chosen for calculations. For the feedstock production and harvesting/collection phase, the functional unit was defined as 1 t of fresh matter. Also, 1 kWh of electricity (kWhe) produced in a CHP using biogas was the functional unit (FU) for the entire electricity pro- duction system. The selection of these FUs is in agreement with other biogas LCA studies (Dressler et al., 2012; Pöschl et al., 2012; Bacenetti et al., 2012a, 2012b).
13: All three bioenergy systems were assessed from a cradle-to-grave approach. Thus, the burdens of all input processes of the entire life cycle from raw material acquisition or generation from natural re- sources were included. The systems under assessment take into account all of the processes (and corresponding inputs) involved throughout the life cycle: the agricultural activities related to the maize cultivation, the transport of maize and pig slurry up to the biogas plant, the produc- tion and use of biogas, and digestate management.
Table 1 summarizes some relevant information concerning the biogas plants assessed and the feedstock digested.

All the three biogas plants are located in the Po Valley area a big plain located in Northern Italy. Po Valley is a suitable area for cultiva- tion with good water availability and an intensive agriculture. The typical farm activities are cereal cultivation and livestock (pigs and cattle). The dominant cultivations are annual crops, mainly cereals (maize, triticale, wheat and rice). Among the arborous crops, the poplar is the most widespread, mostly along the rivers.
All three biogas plants are owned by the farmers that manages also the field activities.

<list>The biogas plant A has an electrical power of 999 kW. It is located in Vercelli district (Piedmont), and it co-digests maize silage and pig slurry. The farm has an agricultural used area (AUA) of 252 ha cultivated with cereals (maize and rice). The maize for the digester is growing in the surrounding farm area, while the pig slurry comes from nearby breeding farms. 
The biogas plant B has a lower electrical power (520 kWe), is located in Pavia district (Lombardy), and is fed only with maize silage pro- duced in the farm area. The farm has an agricultural used area (AUA) of 182 ha. 
The biogas plant C has an electrical power of 250 kW, is located in Lodi district (Lombardy), and is fed only with pig slurry that comes from nearby breeding farms. The farm has an agricultural used area (AUA) of 124 ha cultivated with maize, sorghum and triticale.</list>
Fig. 1 shows the system boundaries for the three bioenergy systems. The biogas-to-electricity system, similarly to other biomass-to-energy systems, can be divided into three phases or subsystems:

<list>1. Subsystem I: Feedstock production. This stage includes the follow- ing processes depending on the scenario: maize cultivation and harvest as well as pig slurry recovery. The production of the pig slurry has been excluded from the system boundaries because it can be considered residual waste from the pig production system. 
2. Subsystem II: Transport and storage. The transport of chopped maize from fields to the AD storage point, the maize ensilage, the handling operation of the maize silage, and the slurry transport from animal husbandry to the AD plant have been included in this subsystem. 
3. Subsystem III: Conversion. Both the transformation of biomass into biogas and biogas into electricity and heat processes have been considered within this subsystem.</list>

17: Table 2 displays the agricultural operations and machinery man- aged in the maize production subsystem (Subsystem I) in the districts of Vercelli and Pavia. In May, before plowing, the soil was previously fertilized with digestate at rates of 75 and 85 t ha for the Pavia and Vercelli districts, respectively. After plowing, always in May, soil is harrowed, sown, and treated with herbicides (6 kg/ha in the Pavia district and 5 kg/ha in the Vercelli district). The sowed seeds range from 70,000 (19 kg/ha) to 77,000 (20 kg/ha) seeds/ha. In June, the following operations are executed: chemical weed control using 1 kg/ha of a pre-emergence herbicide and the mineral fertilization with urea. After the top fertilization, in July and August, irrigation (800 for each intervention) is performed 6 and 5 times in Pavia and Vercelli district, respectively. The harvesting occurs in September with self-propelled machines. More detailed information concerning the maize production subsystem can be found in González-García et al. (2013).
The average biomass yield is higher (70.2 with a dry matter content of 34%) in the Pavia district than it is in Vercelli (65.1 with a dry matter content of 38%).

The digestate utilized for pre-seeding organic fertilization comes from the plant itself (instead of urea). The digestate that originates from the pig slurry is used as a fertilizer in fodder production and hence is excluded from the analysis because this organic fertilizer would also be applied without biogas production.

Table 3 reports the differences regarding nitrogen fertilization for maize growing in the Pavia and Vercelli districts. In both, the fertili- zation is carried out using both mineral and organic fertilizers. N-application ranges between 346 and 367 kg/ha1.

The pig slurry is a byproduct of animal breeding. For this reason, only operations not foreseen in usual slurry management were considered for the energetic and environmental evaluation of the bioenergy systems.

19: At the biogas plant, biomass is stored in silos where it is ensiled. Maize is transported from the field to the biogas plants with farm tipping trailers. The average transport distance between the field and the biogas plant is 3 km and 5 km, respectively, for Pavia and Vercelli districts.

Table 4 reports information about the collection of pig slurry. The transport of pig slurry is done by sludge tanks, considering an average distance of 2 km for the biogas plant located in Vercelli district and 4 km for the plants in Lodi district.

During ensiling and storage operations, some biomass is lost. In this study, a maize loss of 2% of mass was considered. For pig slurry, no losses occur, considering that it is moved through pipelines.
20: Table 5 reports detailed information about the three biogas plants. The three plants assessed in this study operate under mesophilic conditions (40 °C). Single-stage digestion takes place in continuous stirred-tank reactors (CSTR). Inside the digesters, the material (feedstock, water, and liquid fraction) is made homogeneous by means of mixers and pumps. The biogas produced as a result of the digestion of organic matter is stored in the gasholder dome placed on the top of the digester.

Maize silage and pig slurry properties for biogas production are reported in Table 6 (Fiala, 2012). Considering the biomass character- istics, a dilution of the maize silage is required for both A and B plants. In these two plants, inside the digesters, in order to allow for the operation of mixers and pumps, the total solid (dry matter) concentration is 10%. So, in biogas plant A, 1 t of silage maize with TS = 38%, corresponding to 3.8 t of feedstock mixture. Dilution of the feedstock is carried out with liquid fraction coming from digestate and eventually water. One ton of feedstock mixture corresponds with

Fig. 1. System boundaries for the three biogas systems. Note: circular = infrastructure, hexagon = material or energy, rectangular = operations (TE = thermal energy, EE = electric energy, CHP = combine heat and power engine).

0.9 t of digestate. Digestate is partially separated into solid and liquid fractions. The liquid fraction is utilized for dilution, while the solid frac- tion is spread in the maize field as organic fertilizer.
For the biogas plant C (only pig slurry is fed), no dilution is carried out. One ton of pig slurry corresponds to 1 t of feedstock mixture (total solid content 3.5%) and 0.98 t of digestate.

Information concerning daily consumption of feedstock and dilu- tion matrix (water or liquid fraction) as well as the digestate produc- tion yields is reported in Table 7. The biogas extracted from the gasholder dome, before being burnt, is filtered, dehumidified, and desulphurized. After those treatments, the biogas is used in internal combustion engines to generate renewable power via cogeneration in the form of electricity and heat. The combined heat and power (CHP) engines are linked to alternators in order to produce electricity. The CHP recovers the heat from both the engine water cooling jackets (70-90 and exhaust gasses (120-150 °C).

During the conversion phase, an electricity self-consumption equal to 7.5%, 8.7%, and 11.3% of the produced electricity was measured for the biogas plants A, B and C, respectively. This electricity is consumed for biomass loading (pumps for slurry and screw with hopper for silage), for biomass mixing inside the digesters, for biogas treatments (by refrigeration units and scrubbers) and for heat dissipation (dry coolers).
Thermal energy demand for heating the digesters comes from the CHP engine. It has been computed considering:

<list>a) the amount of feedstock, water, and liquid fraction introduced in the digesters; 
b) the difference between the temperature inside the digester (40 °C) and the average local temperature, 
c) a specific heat capacity for feedstock, water, and liquid fraction that is equal to (4 kJ/K kg), 
d) a dispersion of heat from the digester walls and from the gasholder dome equals 20% of the thermal energy required to heat the mate- rials (Rapport et al., 2011).</list>
Net electricity production is fed into the national grid, while net thermal energy production is waste by means of dry-coolers.

The biogas extracted from the gasholder dome, before being burnt, is filtered, dehumidified, and desulphurized. After those treatments, the biogas is used in internal combustion engines to generate renewable power via cogeneration in the form of electricity and heat. The combined heat and power (CHP) engines are linked to alternators in order to produce electricity. The CHP recovers the heat from both the engine water cooling jackets (70-90 and exhaust gasses (120-150 °C).

During the conversion phase, an electricity self-consumption equal to 7.5%, 8.7%, and 11.3% of the produced electricity was measured for the biogas plants A, B and C, respectively. This electricity is consumed for biomass loading (pumps for slurry and screw with hopper for silage), for biomass mixing inside the digesters, for biogas treatments (by refrigeration units and scrubbers) and for heat dissipation (dry coolers).
Thermal energy demand for heating the digesters comes from the CHP engine. It has been computed considering:

<list>a) the amount of feedstock, water, and liquid fraction introduced in the digesters; 
b) the difference between the temperature inside the digester (40 °C) and the average local temperature, 
c) a specific heat capacity for feedstock, water, and liquid fraction that is equal to (4 kJ/K kg), 
d) a dispersion of heat from the digester walls and from the gasholder dome equals 20% of the thermal energy required to heat the mate- rials (Rapport et al., 2011).</list>
Net electricity production is fed into the national grid, while net thermal energy production is waste by means of dry-coolers.
22: Table 5 reports detailed information about the three biogas plants. The three plants assessed in this study operate under mesophilic conditions (40 °C). Single-stage digestion takes place in continuous stirred-tank reactors (CSTR). Inside the digesters, the material (feedstock, water, and liquid fraction) is made homogeneous by means of mixers and pumps. The biogas produced as a result of the digestion of organic matter is stored in the gasholder dome placed on the top of the digester.

Maize silage and pig slurry properties for biogas production are reported in Table 6 (Fiala, 2012). Considering the biomass character- istics, a dilution of the maize silage is required for both A and B plants. In these two plants, inside the digesters, in order to allow for the operation of mixers and pumps, the total solid (dry matter) concentration is 10%. So, in biogas plant A, 1 t of silage maize with TS = 38%, corresponding to 3.8 t of feedstock mixture. Dilution of the feedstock is carried out with liquid fraction coming from digestate and eventually water. One ton of feedstock mixture corresponds with

0.9 t of digestate. Digestate is partially separated into solid and liquid fractions. The liquid fraction is utilized for dilution, while the solid frac- tion is spread in the maize field as organic fertilizer.
For the biogas plant C (only pig slurry is fed), no dilution is carried out. One ton of pig slurry corresponds to 1 t of feedstock mixture (total solid content 3.5%) and 0.98 t of digestate.

24: <header>2.4. Inventory data </header>

The quality of an LCA study depends considerably on the life cycle inventory data managed. In this study, real data have been considered (2011-2012 period) and complemented, when necessary, with sec- ondary data, as described below.

Inventory data concerning the production of maize (machineries, operation hours, etc.) and transport-related activities were collected by means of surveys on farms and by interviews with the farmers (year 2012). As a result, different ranges of views on fuel consump- tion in agricultural activities, on irrigation, on the fertilizer applica- tion, and on the maize biomass yield have been identified (Tables 2 and 3).
Concerning the pig slurry, data regarding transport activities were collected via interviews with the owners of the biogas plants A and C.

As mentioned, three biogas plants have been inventoried and assessed. Information concerning their operation, such as the bio- mass consumptions, biogas production, electricity, and heat require- ment, was achieved through plant monitoring (from November 2011 to October 2012), and average data corresponding to the operation in one year has been managed. In the three plants assessed, biogas losses were considered from digesters and biogas treatment devices (1%) and from the CHP (0.5%) (Dressler et al., 2012).

Information concerning the production of the different agricultural inputs (machinery, mineral fertilizers, seeds, and diesel) as well as up- stream processes, such as power generation, was based on secondary data taken from the Ecoinvent database (Frischknecht et al., 2007; Nemecek and Käggi, 2007; Spielmann et al., 2007). Table 8 summarizes the data sources used for the LCA.
Field emissions (nitrous oxide, ammonia, and nitrate leaching) from fertilizers (organic and mineral) were estimated based on the

amounts of applied nitrogen, following the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) method (IPCC, 2006; Brentrup et al., 2000).

Concerning the carbon stored in the biomass, it was not taken into ac- count because it has been considered to be completely re-emitted, such as CO2, during anaerobic digestion and biogas combustion (Capponi et al., 2012).

Finally, zero change has been assumed in the overall soil carbon con- tent under maize cultivation due to the specific dependency of this on factors such as management practices and climate, and considering that the fields were previously dedicated to cereal cultivation.
2.5. Methods

Energy analysis was performed using the cumulative energy de- mand method (CED). The CED, computed according to Hischier et al. (2009), represents the entire energy demand, valued as primary energy, which arises in connection with production, use, and disposal of an eco- nomic good. In this category, the consumption of energy from different non-renewable sources (fossil and nuclear) and renewable sources is analyzed in detail in terms of the MJ (lower heating value) equivalent all throughout the life cycle of the bioenergy systems under assessment.

The climate change was calculated for a 100-year time frame based on GHG emissions indicated as CO2 equivalents (eq) and defined by the IPCC (2006). The GHG emissions (EMGHG; kg CO2eq/kWhe) derived from the bioenergy systems proposed were calculated, considering that in accordance with the guidelines of IPCC (2006), the CO2 removed from the atmosphere through photosynthesis offsets the CO2 released during anaerobic digestion and biogas combustion.
For the three bioenergy systems, fossil energy balance as well as the GHG balance were drawn as follows:
= (EMFD + EMBP + EMOM + EMML + EMDI)-(CREE + CRFS + CRAD)
Where:
CED Fossil energy balance between fossil energy consumption and fossil energy saved (ES).
EMGHG GHG balance between GHG emitted and GHG credits (CR) for avoided GHG emissions.
CEDFD and EMFD fossil energy consumption and GHG emissions for the production of maize silage (plants A and B) and for pig slurry
recovery (plants A and C). EMFD considers also the emissions due to N20 volatilization from fertilized soil.

Concerning the carbon stored in the biomass, it was not taken into ac- count because it has been considered to be completely re-emitted, such as CO2, during anaerobic digestion and biogas combustion (Capponi et al., 2012).

2.5. Methods

Energy analysis was performed using the cumulative energy de- mand method (CED). The CED, computed according to Hischier et al. (2009), represents the entire energy demand, valued as primary energy, which arises in connection with production, use, and disposal of an eco- nomic good. In this category, the consumption of energy from different non-renewable sources (fossil and nuclear) and renewable sources is analyzed in detail in terms of the MJ (lower heating value) equivalent all throughout the life cycle of the bioenergy systems under assessment.

The climate change was calculated for a 100-year time frame based on GHG emissions indicated as CO2 equivalents (eq) and defined by the IPCC (2006). The GHG emissions (EMGHG; kg CO2eq/kWhe) derived from the bioenergy systems proposed were calculated, considering that in accordance with the guidelines of IPCC (2006), the CO2 removed from the atmosphere through photosynthesis offsets the CO2 released during anaerobic digestion and biogas combustion.
For the three bioenergy systems, fossil energy balance as well as the GHG balance were drawn as follows:
= (EMFD + EMBP + EMOM + EMML + EMDI)-(CREE + CRFS + CRAD)
Where:
CED Fossil energy balance between fossil energy consumption and fossil energy saved (ES).
EMGHG GHG balance between GHG emitted and GHG credits (CR) for avoided GHG emissions.
CEDFD and EMFD fossil energy consumption and GHG emissions for the production of maize silage (plants A and B) and for pig slurry
recovery (plants A and C). EMFD considers also the emissions due to N20 volatilization from fertilized soil.
27: <header>2.4. Inventory data </header>

The quality of an LCA study depends considerably on the life cycle inventory data managed. In this study, real data have been considered (2011-2012 period) and complemented, when necessary, with sec- ondary data, as described below.

Inventory data concerning the production of maize (machineries, operation hours, etc.) and transport-related activities were collected by means of surveys on farms and by interviews with the farmers (year 2012). As a result, different ranges of views on fuel consump- tion in agricultural activities, on irrigation, on the fertilizer applica- tion, and on the maize biomass yield have been identified (Tables 2 and 3).
Concerning the pig slurry, data regarding transport activities were collected via interviews with the owners of the biogas plants A and C.

As mentioned, three biogas plants have been inventoried and assessed. Information concerning their operation, such as the bio- mass consumptions, biogas production, electricity, and heat require- ment, was achieved through plant monitoring (from November 2011 to October 2012), and average data corresponding to the operation in one year has been managed. In the three plants assessed, biogas losses were considered from digesters and biogas treatment devices (1%) and from the CHP (0.5%) (Dressler et al., 2012).

Information concerning the production of the different agricultural inputs (machinery, mineral fertilizers, seeds, and diesel) as well as up- stream processes, such as power generation, was based on secondary data taken from the Ecoinvent database (Frischknecht et al., 2007; Nemecek and Käggi, 2007; Spielmann et al., 2007). Table 8 summarizes the data sources used for the LCA.

2.5. Methods

Energy analysis was performed using the cumulative energy de- mand method (CED). The CED, computed according to Hischier et al. The climate change was calculated for a 100-year time frame based on GHG emissions indicated as CO2 equivalents (eq) and defined by the IPCC (2006). The GHG emissions (EMGHG; kg CO2eq/kWhe) derived from the bioenergy systems proposed were calculated, considering that in accordance with the guidelines of IPCC (2006), the CO2 removed from the atmosphere through photosynthesis offsets the CO2 released during anaerobic digestion and biogas combustion.
For the three bioenergy systems, fossil energy balance as well as the GHG balance were drawn as follows:
= (EMFD + EMBP + EMOM + EMML + EMDI)-(CREE + CRFS + CRAD)
Where:
CED Fossil energy balance between fossil energy consumption and fossil energy saved (ES).
EMGHG GHG balance between GHG emitted and GHG credits (CR) for avoided GHG emissions.
CEDFD and EMFD fossil energy consumption and GHG emissions for the production of maize silage (plants A and B) and for pig slurry
recovery (plants A and C). EMFD considers also the emissions due to N20 volatilization from fertilized soil.
30: <header>2.4. Inventory data </header>

The quality of an LCA study depends considerably on the life cycle inventory data managed. In this study, real data have been considered (2011-2012 period) and complemented, when necessary, with sec- ondary data, as described below.

Inventory data concerning the production of maize (machineries, operation hours, etc.) and transport-related activities were collected by means of surveys on farms and by interviews with the farmers (year 2012). As a result, different ranges of views on fuel consump- tion in agricultural activities, on irrigation, on the fertilizer applica- tion, and on the maize biomass yield have been identified (Tables 2 and 3).
Concerning the pig slurry, data regarding transport activities were collected via interviews with the owners of the biogas plants A and C.

As mentioned, three biogas plants have been inventoried and assessed. Information concerning their operation, such as the bio- mass consumptions, biogas production, electricity, and heat require- ment, was achieved through plant monitoring (from November 2011 to October 2012), and average data corresponding to the operation in one year has been managed. In the three plants assessed, biogas losses were considered from digesters and biogas treatment devices (1%) and from the CHP (0.5%) (Dressler et al., 2012).

Information concerning the production of the different agricultural inputs (machinery, mineral fertilizers, seeds, and diesel) as well as up- stream processes, such as power generation, was based on secondary data taken from the Ecoinvent database (Frischknecht et al., 2007; Nemecek and Käggi, 2007; Spielmann et al., 2007). Table 8 summarizes the data sources used for the LCA.

Information concerning their operation, such as the bio- mass consumptions, biogas production, electricity, and heat require- ment, was achieved through plant monitoring (from November 2011 to October 2012), and average data corresponding to the operation in one year has been managed. In the three plants assessed, biogas losses were considered from digesters and biogas treatment devices (1%) and from the CHP (0.5%) (Dressler et al., 2012).

Information concerning the production of the different agricultural inputs (machinery, mineral fertilizers, seeds, and diesel) as well as up- stream processes, such as power generation, was based on secondary data taken from the Ecoinvent database (Frischknecht et al., 2007; Nemecek and Käggi, 2007; Spielmann et al., 2007). Table 8 summarizes the data sources used for the LCA.

amounts of applied nitrogen, following the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) method (IPCC, 2006; Brentrup et al., 2000).

Concerning the carbon stored in the biomass, it was not taken into ac- count because it has been considered to be completely re-emitted, such as CO2, during anaerobic digestion and biogas combustion (Capponi et al., 2012).

Finally, zero change has been assumed in the overall soil carbon con- tent under maize cultivation due to the specific dependency of this on factors such as management practices and climate, and considering that the fields were previously dedicated to cereal cultivation.
2.5. Methods

Energy analysis was performed using the cumulative energy de- mand method (CED). The CED, computed according to Hischier et al. (2009), represents the entire energy demand, valued as primary energy, which arises in connection with production, use, and disposal of an eco- nomic good. In this category, the consumption of energy from different non-renewable sources (fossil and nuclear) and renewable sources is analyzed in detail in terms of the MJ (lower heating value) equivalent all throughout the life cycle of the bioenergy systems under assessment.

The climate change was calculated for a 100-year time frame based on GHG emissions indicated as CO2 equivalents (eq) and defined by the IPCC (2006). The GHG emissions (EMGHG; kg CO2eq/kWhe) derived from the bioenergy systems proposed were calculated, considering that in accordance with the guidelines of IPCC (2006), the CO2 removed from the atmosphere through photosynthesis offsets the CO2 released during anaerobic digestion and biogas combustion.
For the three bioenergy systems, fossil energy balance as well as the GHG balance were drawn as follows:
= (EMFD + EMBP + EMOM + EMML + EMDI)-(CREE + CRFS + CRAD)
Where:
CED Fossil energy balance between fossil energy consumption and fossil energy saved (ES).
EMGHG GHG balance between GHG emitted and GHG credits (CR) for avoided GHG emissions.
CEDFD and EMFD fossil energy consumption and GHG emissions for the production of maize silage (plants A and B) and for pig slurry
recovery (plants A and C). EMFD considers also the emissions due to N20 volatilization from fertilized soil.

ESEE and CREE fossil energy consumption and GHG emissions for the electricity production in the reference system. This fossil energy is saved by producing electricity from renewable sources and, then, is credited to the biogas bioenergy sys- tem. On the other hand, for the same reason, these GHG emissions are avoided and, therefore credited to the bio- energy system. The reference system (RS) is the electric energy generation from fossil fuel in Italy [2218 kWh/kWhe; 541.7 g CO2eq/kWhe] (ISPRA, 2011). An additional reference system for electricity production has been utilized as an alternative scenario.

In the three investigated bioenergy systems, electricity is the main product of a biogas system, but besides it, the CHP engine also produces thermal energy. Moreover, from the digesters, a considerable amount of digestate is taken away every day.

ESTE and CRTE fossil energy consumption and GHG emissions for the heat from fossil fuel in the reference system. This energy and these emissions are "credits" granted to the bioenergy system for the substitution of heat produced from fossil fuels with thermal energy cogenerated by the CHP. Heat is a co-product of the biogas system and can be used in order to substitute thermal energy produced with fossil fuel. Nevertheless, in the three biogas plants, heat is used only for the thermal supply of digesters. Surplus heat is wasted, so no credits are considered for this byproduct. However, considering the revision of the incentives for bio- gas in Italy (Ministero Sviluppo Economico, 2012), the im- pact of thermal energy valorization on energetic and GHG emission balance was assessed separately as an alternative scenario (AS).

of fertilizers substituted. For the three biogas systems, ESFS and CRFS are considered equal to zero because: i) the digestate produced in plants A and B is completely utilized as organic fertilizer for maize pre-seeding fertilization and ii) the amount produced by the pig slurry is used as fertilizer in fodder production and, hence, is excluded from the analysis because this organic fertilizer would be applied also without biogas production. represents credits attributed to the bioenergy system for the anaerobic digestion of manure. AD of slurry reduces methane emissions compared with storage in an uncovered tank (IPCC, 2006). In this study, in accordance with Lansche and Müller (2012), it has been assumed that these emis- sions are equal to 16% of pig slurry potential biogas produc- tion. The impact of the exclusion of those credits on EMGH has been assessed in an alternative scenario.
ESFS and CRFS credits granted when the digestate replaces mineral fer- tilizers. These credits are equal to fossil energy consumption and to GHG emissions linked to the production of the amount

However, considering the revision of the incentives for bio- gas in Italy (Ministero Sviluppo Economico, 2012), the im- pact of thermal energy valorization on energetic and GHG emission balance was assessed separately as an alternative scenario (AS).

of fertilizers substituted. For the three biogas systems, ESFS and CRFS are considered equal to zero because: i) the digestate produced in plants A and B is completely utilized as organic fertilizer for maize pre-seeding fertilization and ii) the amount produced by the pig slurry is used as fertilizer in fodder production and, hence, is excluded from the analysis because this organic fertilizer would be applied also without biogas production. represents credits attributed to the bioenergy system for the anaerobic digestion of manure. AD of slurry reduces methane emissions compared with storage in an uncovered tank (IPCC, 2006). 40: In the following section, the performances of the three bioenergy systems previously described (henceforth called the baseline scenario or BS) are reported and discussed in detail. The results for feedstock production (maize production and pig slurry recovery) and transport as well as for biomass transformation into biogas and into electricity are given.
41: Results corresponding to the assessment of silage maize produc- tion and pig slurry recovery are shown in Table 9 in terms of fossil en- ergy consumption (CEDFD) and GHG emissions (EMFD).

For maize silage production, differences between the two investi- gated areas exist. The Vercelli district has the greatest GHG emissions, while the greatest energy demand occurs in Pavia district. These dif- ferences are mainly due to the biomass yields: Maize production is higher in Pavia than it is in the Vercelli district.

The GHG emissions associated with maize cultivation range from 78.6 kg CO2eq/t to 82.7 kg CO2eq/t of fresh maize (twb). The lowest GHG emissions occur in the district of Pavia because of the higher yield. For the same reason, in the district of Vercelli, the CED for the maize cultivation is higher than it is in Pavia.
The mechanization of field operations plays an important role in the definition of an environmental load; the 65% total GHG emissions are due to machine use along with fuel and lubricant consumption.

Nitrous emissions from soil represent an important GHG emis- sions source: Direct and indirect nitrous emissions reach 25% and 23% of the total GHG emissions in the Vercelli and Pavia districts, re- spectively. In the Vercelli district, despite the lower use of mineral fertilizers (60 kg/ha vs 100 kg/ha of urea), the nitrous oxide (N20) emissions are higher due to the greater nitrogen application by means of digestate (85 t/year).

The total fossil energy demand associated with maize production ranges from 736.8 MJ/twb to 769.2 MJ/twb. In the district of Pavia, the application of a higher-mineral fertilizer is offset by increased yield, and the CED is slightly lower than it is in the Vercelli district. For hectare, CED is equal to 49.99 GJ in Vercelli and 51.60 GJ in the Pavia district.

The mechanization of field operations (including fuel and lubri- cant consumption) requires a total fossil energy demand that is sim- ilar for the Pavia and Vercelli districts. Between the two areas, this energy represents an important share (77% and 79%, respectively) of the total energy demand.
For pig slurry, the energy demand ranges from 92.3 MJ/twb in the Lodi district to 156.9 MJ/twb in the Vercelli district, while the GHG

emissions is 6.6 CO2eq/twb (Lodi district) to 11.14 kg CO2eq/twb (Vercelli district). Considering that in the slurry is transported via a tractor coupled with sludge tanks (volume 12 m³) in both of these districts, the GHG emissions as well as the energy demand mainly depend on the transport distance.

44: Digesters are daily fed with the different feedstocks. The feeding is carried out continuously in order to achieve a stable biogas pro- duction. A stable biogas production allows the engine to run at full power.

Considering losses during loading (transport from the silos to the digesters are carried out by a tractor with a frontal loader), 32.3 twb/day and 52.9 twb/day of maize silage are introduced in the biogas plants in the Pavia and Vercelli districts, respectively. At the same time, 45 twb/day of pig slurry are introduced in the di- gesters in the plant located in the Vercelli district (plant A) because it co-digests pig slurry together with maize silage. Concerning the plant C located in Lodi district, 210 twb/day of pig slurry are fed in the digesters. Table 10 reports the LHV (Lower Heating Value) of the biogas, the daily biogas flow, and the corresponding methane losses.
45: The combustion of the biogas in the CHP engine entails the simulta- neous generation of electricity and heat. The AD plant self-consumes a share of this energy, both electric and thermal, for its operation (mixers, pumps, and refrigeration units).

The electricity gross production, self-consumption, and net produc- tion (fed into the national grid) per day are reported in Table 11. Electric self-consumption is quite similar for the three biogas plants, and it ranges from 7.33% to 9.50% of the gross production. Thermal energy self-consumption is variable among the three plants, and it is consider- ably higher (93.2% of gross production) for plant C fed only with pig slurry. For the two plants (A and B) fed with maize silage (feedstock with high, specific production of biogas) the heat self-consumption is lower (around 30% of gross production).

Specific heat consumption for biogas is 0.726 kWh,/m3, 0.707 kWh,/m³ and 2.846 kWh,/m³ N, while, for electricity, it is 0.373 kWh,/kWh., 0.413 kWh,/kWhe, and 1.462 kWh,/kWhe in plants fed with energy crop and byproduct, plants fed only with silage maize, and plants fed only with pig slurry, respectively. Table 12 reports the results for the CED and GHG balance assessments.
Table 12 CED and GHG emission balance for the three biogas bioenergy systems.

plant Biogas|Biogas production|Lower heating value|Biogas utilized|Methane losses plant Biogas|m3/N/day|kWh/m³|kWh/m³|m3/N/day Vercelli A|11,438|5.21|11,266|91.6 Pavia B|6590|5.18|6491|52.4 Lodi C|2811|6.06|2769|26.1

The results reported for the assessment of total fossil energy de- mand indicate that they are better for the biogas plants fed with maize silage (plants A and B) than the ones for the plant fed with pig slurry (Plant C). Regarding the energetic point of view, the performance of plant C located in the Lodi district (fed with pig slurry) is considerably worse than it is for the other two biogas plants. However, considering the credits for electricity generation, the three bioenergy systems have positive performance under an energy perspective that is also without thermal energy utilization (see Table 12).
The two plants fed with silage maize show very similar CED performance 5.69 MJ/kWhe in plant A and 5.63 MJ/kWhe in plant B). Plant C (fed with pig slurry) shows the worst performance 3.97 MJ/kWhe).

If it is analyzed in more detail in terms of the CED, the key as- pects are the feedstock production and transport, with a share of 91.6%, 91.8%, and 98.2% of the total fossil energy demand for the plants A, B, and C, respectively. Concerning Plant A, which codigests maize, silage, and pig slurry, the pig slurry transport (carried out with a slurry tank) represents 22% of the total fossil energy demand but produces only 3.6% of the biogas volume.

Compared with the fossil reference system, all of the three biogas biosystems reduce the GHG emissions for electricity production. All of the three biogas-to-electricity systems achieve a considerable GHG emission savings. Despite the CED, Plant C, completely fed with pig slurry, shows the best performance mainly due to the credits for AD of slurry (CRAD). For the two biogas plants, fed mainly with maize silage, the GHG emission savings are similar (0.286 kg CO2eq/kWhe for plant A and 0.230 kg CO2eq/kWhe for plant B).

The differences for the EMGHG are mainly due to the credits granting for AD of manure, the feedstock production, the transport distances, and the biogas plant characteristics (self-consumption, CHP efficiency). The credits for anaerobic digestion of manure strongly influence the EMGHG result. That is, despite the higher GHG emissions for production and re- covery of feedstock, the best environmental impact occurs in Plant fed only with pig slurry. Also, between the plants fed with silage maize, the differences in EMGHG are linked to those credits (CRAD) that have been granted for Plant A (that also codigests pig slurry) but not for Plant B (fed only with maize silage).

Within that impact category, the GHG emissions for feedstock cultiva- tion and recovery ranges from 0.294 (Plant A) to 0.350 kg CO2eq/kWhe (Plant C). For plants A and B, maize cultivation is the main GHG emission source (59-66% of total). On the contrary, in the biogas plant fed with pig slurry (Plant C), the pig slurry recovery contributes with 76% of GHG emissions. Also, methane losses in the biogas subsystems (1.5% of the produced volume) play a key role, comprising 23%, 29%, and 30% of the overall emissions in plants A, B, and C, respectively.
46: The combustion of the biogas in the CHP engine entails the simulta- neous generation of electricity and heat. The AD plant self-consumes a share of this energy, both electric and thermal, for its operation (mixers, pumps, and refrigeration units).

The electricity gross production, self-consumption, and net produc- tion (fed into the national grid) per day are reported in Table 11. Electric self-consumption is quite similar for the three biogas plants, and it ranges from 7.33% to 9.50% of the gross production. Thermal energy self-consumption is variable among the three plants, and it is consider- ably higher (93.2% of gross production) for plant C fed only with pig slurry. For the two plants (A and B) fed with maize silage (feedstock with high, specific production of biogas) the heat self-consumption is lower (around 30% of gross production).

Specific heat consumption for biogas is 0.726 kWh,/m3, 0.707 kWh,/m³ and 2.846 kWh,/m³ N, while, for electricity, it is 0.373 kWh,/kWh., 0.413 kWh,/kWhe, and 1.462 kWh,/kWhe in plants fed with energy crop and byproduct, plants fed only with silage maize, and plants fed only with pig slurry, respectively. 53: The replacement of fossil fuels with energy from biomass allows one to mitigate fossil CO2 emissions as well as to preserve the future availability of fossil fuels (von Blottnitz and Curran, 2007; Mizey and Racz, 2010).

Nevertheless, it is essential that the production of the feedstock is carried out under sustainable conditions. The methodologies for the assessment of energetic and environmental performances of bioenergy systems are not free of uncertainty and variability. Numerous authors have used the LCA methodology in studies where a huge range of bioenergy systems had to be evaluated in detail (Fazio and Monti, 2011; González-García et al., 2012, 2013).

In Italy (specifically in the Po Valley), in the region under assess- ment in this paper, several energy crops are already cultivated; poplar plantations are cultivated for thermochemical conversion; and annual crops, such as wheat, triticale, and maize, for biogas production (González-García et al., 2013).
54: For the three biogas-to-electricity biosystems, three alternative scenarios (AS) have been proposed and evaluated.

In this scenario (AS1), the impact of heat valorization on energetic and environmental performances has been assessed using the credit approach. The credits were granted to the biogas system, assuming full valorization of net thermal energy. Those credits are equal to the primary energy consumption and to the GHG emissions entailed by a fossil-based energy generation plant.

Therefore, 1.25 kWhfossil/kWht and 0.226 kg CO2eq/kWht are credited to the biogas system for net thermal energy. These values represent the primary energy consumption and the GHG emissions for large gas boilers (Bini and Magistro, 2002; Bottio et al., 2008; Capponi et al., 2012).
55: No credits for AD have been taken into account in this scenario (AS2) in order to assess the impact of AD of manure on environmen- tal performance.
56: For electricity production, in this scenario (AS3), a different refer- ence system (RS) has been considered. During the past few decades, in Italy, regarding the fossil source, the generation has moved from carbon to natural gas. Thus, for this RS, it has been assumed that the electricity from biogas substitutes the electric energy produced mainly from carbon. In detail, the electricity production from natural-gas-fired power plants (30%) and from coal-fired power plants (70%) has been considered to be the RS (Bachmaier et al., 2010), thus resulting in a CED that is equal to 2.55 kWh/kWhe and GHG emissions of 0.825 kg CO2eq/kWhe.
The results for ASs are reported in Table 13. Fig. 2 shows the same results in comparison with BS.

In comparison with the fossil RS, the electricity production using biogas saves GHG emissions from 0.188 kg CO2eq/kWhe to 1.193 kg CO2eq/kWhe. Electricity supply from biogas can also contribute to a considerable reduction of the use of fossil energy carriers (from 3.97 MJ/kWhe to 5.69 MJ/kWhe). The differences are mainly due to credit granting. Without the accounting of credits for electricity from biogas, net GHG emission varies from 0.292 kg CO2eq/kWhe to 0.354 kg CO2eq/kWhe and energy con- sumption from 2.29 MJfossil/kWhe to 4.01 MJfossil/kWhe.
The comparison among the different scenarios highlights that, with respect to the BS:

<list>i) The granting of CRET entails a strong improvement of fossil ener- gy savings for biogas plants fed with silage maize 63% plant A, + 79% plant B) and a lower reduction for the plant fed only with pig slurry 12%). Similar results are achieved regarding EMGHG: The improvement is equal to + 70%, + 108%, and + 3% for plants A, B, and C, respectively. The lower impact on Plant C is due to higher heat self-consumption; 
ii) The credits for AD of manure have a big impact on the GHG emission balance for the two plants (A and C) where the di- gesters are fed with pig slurry. In Plant C, without those credits, the GHG emission reduction is considerably lower (-79%) than it is in BS. Do not take into consideration that the CRAD has no effects on the performance of biogas Plant B because no slurry is digested in this plant; 
iii) The use of a different RS for electricity production has a great- er effect on EMGHG than it does on CED. In AS3, considering an electricity generation based mainly on carbon as fossil fuel, CREE is increased regarding BS. This increase is reflected di- rectly in an enhancement of CED 21% for plants A and C, -30% for Plant C) and EMGH performance +99%, + 123% and + 31% for biogas plant A, B, and C, respectively).</list>
Comparing the three biogas plants, it can be stated that:

<list>i) CED is not necessarily correlated with GHG emissions. This is due to the fact that methane and N20 emissions have high-global warming potential but hardly any influence on the energy balance of the biogas system. In details, the biogas Plant C always shows the worse performance in terms of the energetic point of view, while, for the EMGHG, the higher reduction of GHG emissions (from 2 times to 3 times higher, regarding plants A and B) is achieved when CRAD are considered; 
ii) The performances differ considerably between the biogas plants fed with maize silage (A and B) and the biogas plant in which only pig slurry is utilized (C). Although CED and EMGHG are similar for biogas plants A and B, the plant fed only with maize silage (B) also achieves a lower reduction of GHG emissions when the credits for AD of manure are not granted. 
iii) The transport of feedstock with low total solid content and, consequently, low specific biogas production, involves high GHG emissions and substantial energy consumption. Further- more, compared with the biogas plants fed also with silage</list>
Feedstock Other materials Credits TE Credits EE
maize, the plant fed with pig slurry has a considerable higher heat demand.

The result of our study are in accordance with the literature (Bachmaier et al., 2013; Pöschl et al., 2010, 2012; Wulfa et al., 2006; Bacenetti et al., 2012a, 2012b; Schumacher et al., 2010; Meyer-Aurich al., 2012), which shows that electricity production from biogas has huge potential for energy savings and a reduction of GHG emissions.

Compared to our results, Meyer-Aurich et al. (2012) reports lower reductions of GHG emission for electricity from biogas when the land use change (from grassland to maize cultivation) is considered. Bachmaier et al. (2010) show higher energy savings and GHG emission reductions. These differences are due to the use of a different reference system and, consequently, to diverse credits granted for electricity and heat production from fossil fuels.

61: The objective of this study was the assessment of energetic and environmental performances of three biogas plants with different electrical powers using different feedstocks for digesters feeding (maize silage and pig slurry combinations). The reduction of GHG emissions and the saving of fossil energy are important criteria in the context of the biogas assessment. The possibil- ity of limiting global warming is strictly linked to the reduction of GHG

emissions. The saving of fossil fuel allows the reduction of emissions and lets a delay in its depletion. According to our study regardless the feedstock, electricity produc- tion from biogas has huge potential for both energy savings and GHG emissions reductions. Our results reported higher values in terms of reduction of GHG emis- sion when the land use change (from grassland to maize cultivation) was taken into account, in comparison with other studies. Differences on energy savings and GHG emission are mainly due to the use of a different reference system and, consequently, to diverse granted credits for electricity and heat production from fossil fuels. Efficient utilization of the cogenerated heat can substantially im- prove the GHG balance of electricity production from biogas. Further reductions of GHG emissions can be achieved by using an animal waste as feedstock. Manure as feedstock can partially substitute en- ergy crops and entails high credits for the avoided GHG emissions from undigested manure. On the other hand, compared with silage maize, the use of pig slurry involves higher thermal energy consump- tion for heating the digesters and, consequently, lower credits for the heat valorization.

When maize is utilized, energy consumption and the GHG emissions occur mainly due to energy crop production and transport. The im- provement of the bioenergy system sustainability cannot avoid facing issues such as: the reduction of N20 emissions from crop cultivation, the replacement of mineral fertilizers (energy-intensive), and the min- imization of transport distances.

The transport distance plays the major role in energetic and envi- ronmental performances when the biogas plant is fed with feedstock characterized by low specific biogas production (pig slurry). It must be highlighted that, in the future, due to the fast and considerable diffu- sion of biogas plants in some districts, these transport distances might be higher both for maize and pig slurry.

Regarding the biogas plant, the main factors for improving the CED and GHG emission performances are as follows: As much as possible, the heat cogenerated should be used to substitute heat generation from fossil sources, and methane emissions should be minimized along with electricity self-consumption.
